policy_statement: DOS and the Code Council should adopt State codes by 2025 prohibiting fossil-fuel combustion systems/equipment in new single-family and low-rise multifamily buildings (≤3 stories), and by 2028 prohibit fossil-fuel combustion systems/equipment in new commercial and larger multifamily buildings (≥4 stories).
mechanism_description: Adopts state building codes that prohibit fossil-fuel combustion systems in new construction, shifting new buildings to non-combustion equipment and avoiding on-site fossil fuel emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy service provision → electrified/non-combustion end-use technologies → ↓ on-site fossil fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Building design and equipment selection in new construction is constrained/redirected by code so that furnaces/boilers/gas water heaters/gas cooking and other combustion systems are not installed, and electric heat pumps/induction/electric resistance (where used) or other non-combustion alternatives are installed instead.  
(2) Mediating process: Eliminating on-site combustion removes direct CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions from end-use fuel burning and also reduces upstream emissions from fossil fuel extraction/processing/distribution; any remaining emissions occur at electricity generation, which can be lower and can decline as the grid decarbonizes.  
(3) Outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions from building operations compared to fossil-equipped new buildings.
dominant_pathway_test: By prohibiting fossil-fuel combustion equipment in new buildings, the biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from avoided on-site fossil fuel combustion (and associated upstream fuel supply emissions).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Code-driven electrification of end uses → avoids on-site fossil combustion → lowers GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: The principal intended and largest climate effect is emissions reduction, not primarily reduced resource intensity per se (Resource Efficiency) nor hazard reduction (Adaptation) nor ecosystem-mediated benefits (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If the grid is highly carbon-intensive or if electric resistance replaces efficient combustion without efficiency safeguards, near-term net GHG benefits could be smaller; however the mechanism’s dominant intent and typical net effect remains emissions avoidance from combustion and enabling decarbonization via cleaner electricity.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy with this mechanism—prohibiting fossil combustion systems in new buildings via code—acts primarily through operational emissions mitigation by preventing direct fuel combustion.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., “building electrification,” “fuel switching,” “code and standards”) and any efficiency/co-benefit tagging depend on policy text details (scope, end uses covered, compliance paths, grid assumptions) and are deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge cases include limited exemptions (e.g., for process loads, backup generation, commercial cooking), partial bans (space heating only), or contexts with very high-carbon electricity where near-term emissions reductions are reduced or delayed; these do not change the primary mitigation classification for the mechanism.
key_indicators: “Adopt State codes”; “prohibit building systems or equipment used for the combustion of fossil fuels”; “in new construction statewide”; “file for a building permit starting in 2025/2028”; “single-family and low-rise multifamily”; “commercial and larger multifamily”
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health benefits from reduced indoor/outdoor combustion pollutants; potential safety benefits (reduced gas leak/fire risk); possible energy bill stability—mentioned implicitly by electrification but not required for secondary categorization.
instance_edge_case_notes: While structured as time-phased targets (2025/2028), the operative mechanism is a binding prohibition via building code (mandate), not a voluntary target-setting or incentive approach. Electricity-sector emissions dependence is an implementation consideration but does not change the instrument classification.
